Title: Tissue-specific regulation of lipid polyester synthesis genes controlling oxygen permeation into Lotus japonicus nodules
Journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to formation of a hydrophobic barrier restricting oxygen diffusion into nodules
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate positive regulator associated with induction of lipid polyester biosynthesis genes in infected nodules
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative transcriptomics and qRT-PCR induction during rhizobial inoculation
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Up-regulated in infected nodules; induced in inoculated roots
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LjNAC was identified among genes coexpressed with lipid polyester biosynthesis genes in infected nodules. 2. It is homologous to an Arabidopsis NAC linked to suberin biosynthesis. 3. qRT-PCR confirmed induction during root nodule symbiosis.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although a transcription factor, it was not functionally validated in this study as a decisive core regulator; included as a candidate metabolism-related core gene discussed in Results
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential regulatory target for coordinated activation of nodule barrier biosynthesis, but requires direct functional validation

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports barrier formation by likely exporting lipid polyester monomers to the cell wall
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate transporter associated with infected-nodule induction of lipid polyester deposition
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LjABCG2 was identified among infected nodule-upregulated genes. 2. It is homologous to Arabidopsis ABCG2 implicated in export of suberin monomers. 3. The study proposes it may contribute to lipid polyester deposition in nodules.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Functional role remains inferred from homology and coexpression
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential companion target for engineering barrier polymer deposition with biosynthetic enzymes

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Likely contributes to export of lipid polyester precursors for diffusion barrier formation
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate transporter associated with nodule lipid polyester deposition
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LjABCG1 was identified among lipid polyester-related genes induced in infected nodules. 2. It is proposed to participate in export of hydrophobic monomers to the cell wall.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Candidate only; no direct mutant validation in this paper
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential auxiliary target in stacking strategies with FAR genes

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: May assist assembly/remodeling of extracellular lipid polyester barrier
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate cell-wall lipid polyester-related factor induced in infected nodules
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LjGDSL was identified among genes associated with lipid polyester deposition in infected nodules. 2. The study proposes a role in suberin or related wall polymer deposition.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function inferred from annotation; no direct validation here
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible supportive target for polymer assembly engineering

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Likely generates omega-hydroxylated fatty acid monomers for hydrophobic barrier formation that restricts oxygen diffusion
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Candidate biosynthetic gene induced in infected nodules and during compatible nodulation
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Up-regulated in infected nodules; peak expression at 14 dpi after inoculation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LjCYP86A1 was among the strongest lipid polyester-related candidates induced in infected nodules. 2. It is homologous to Arabidopsis HORST/CYP86A1 involved in suberin monomer biosynthesis. 3. qRT-PCR validated induction during nodulation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct biochemical or genetic validation in Lotus nodules was not performed in this paper
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential pathway-stacking target with FAR3.2 to enhance nodule barrier biosynthesis

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Aliphatic suberin monomer formation in nodule endodermis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces fatty acyl-CoA substrates to primary fatty alcohols, key precursors for lipid polyester/suberin formation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Lipid polyester (likely suberin) in the nodule endodermis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Forms a hydrophobic diffusion barrier that restricts oxygen entry into nodules, protecting oxygen-labile nitrogenase and enabling efficient symbiotic nitrogen fixation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes deposition of lipid polyesters in the nodule endodermis; loss of function decreases Fluorol Yellow staining, increases nodule permeability to solutes and oxygen, elevates inner-cortex oxygen, reduces nitrogenase activity, and impairs shoot growth under nitrogen limitation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control point for nodule endodermal barrier formation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: LORE1 loss-of-function mutant analysis; promoter-GUS and promoter-NLS-2xYFP reporters; Fluorol Yellow/Nile red staining; toluidine blue permeability assay; oxygen microelectrode measurements; acetylene reduction assay; growth phenotyping
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: LjFAR3.2 native 3 kb promoter; tissue-specific/ nodule endodermis-enriched
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Nodule-specific/enriched; induced in infected nodules and strongly expressed in mature nodules
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Primarily active in the nodule endodermis; induced in rhizobia-inoculated roots/nodules, peaking during symbiosis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Rhizobial inoculation; nitrogen-limiting growth conditions; nodule symbiosis with Mesorhizobium loti MAFF303099 or subcompatible Rhizobium leguminosarum Norway
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LjFAR3.2 was identified by comparative transcriptomics as up-regulated in infected versus noninfected nodules. 2. Its promoter activity was largely restricted to the nodule endodermis. 3. far3.2 mutants showed reduced lipid polyester deposition in this cell layer. 4. Mutant nodules became more permeable to toluidine blue and oxygen. 5. Increased internal oxygen correlated with lower nitrogenase activity and reduced shoot growth.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss of function compromises nitrogen fixation and plant growth under nitrogen limitation; possible mild effects on nodulation/infection thread number in one allele
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The authors infer the deposited polymer is likely suberin, but note that additional chemical characterization is needed; redundancy with other FAR3 paralogs such as FAR3.4 and FAR3.6 remains possible
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A candidate target to engineer or reinforce nodule oxygen diffusion barriers for improved symbiotic nitrogen fixation; should be manipulated with tissue-specific expression to avoid unwanted barrier effects in other tissues; may be combined with other lipid polyester biosynthesis genes

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Aliphatic suberin/lipid polyester monomer formation in root and nodule endodermal tissues
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Reduces fatty acyl-CoA substrates to primary fatty alcohols used as precursors of lipid polyesters such as suberin
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Root endodermis; nodule vascular endodermis; nodule endodermis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Contributes to hydrophobic wall modifications associated with diffusion barriers
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with lipid polyester deposition in roots and nodules, but tested far3.1 mutants did not show a significant reduction in nodule Fluorol Yellow staining relative to controls
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative transcriptomics; qRT-PCR; phylogenetic analysis; promoter-GUS and promoter-NLS-2xYFP reporter assays; LORE1 mutant characterization with Fluorol Yellow staining
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: LjFAR3.1 native 3 kb promoter; tissue-specific in root and nodule endodermal tissues
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Expressed in roots and inoculated roots; induced in nodules
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Promoter active in root endodermis, nodule vascular endodermis, and nodule endodermis; higher at earlier symbiotic stages than LjFAR3.2 in qRT-PCR
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. LjFAR3.1 was identified among nodule-induced lipid polyester-related genes. 2. Its promoter is active in both root and nodule endodermal tissues. 3. Expression pattern differs from LjFAR3.2, supporting functional diversification of FAR3 paralogs. 4. far3.1 mutants did not show a detectable reduction in nodule lipid polyester staining under the conditions tested.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Likely functionally redundant with other FAR3 paralogs in nodules; may mediate root as well as nodule barrier deposition
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential component for engineering barrier formation, but weaker direct evidence than LjFAR3.2; likely better used in combination with other paralogs
